message:
chore(beads): update issue tracker with new task entries

Add new issue entries and update existing priority/status fields in the
beads issue tracker. These changes reflect the current project planning
state — new tasks for determinism testing, layout correctness, and
property-based testing coverage have been tracked to align with the
implementation work landing in parallel commits.

Co-Authored-By: Claude <noreply@anthropic.com>

 {"id":"bd-2xl.13","title":"Establish cross-platform determinism matrix and drift detector","description":"Guarantee deterministic rendering behavior across Linux/macOS/Windows and common architecture targets.

Scope:
1. Add deterministic snapshot jobs on a cross-platform CI matrix.
2. Normalize known nondeterministic metadata fields from SVG outputs before comparison.
3. Run repeated render loops to detect nondeterministic drift over N iterations.
4. Publish deterministic fingerprints (hashes) as CI artifacts for triage.
5. Fail CI on unexplained output drift with artifact links.

Why this improves the system:
- Protects one of the project's core promises: identical input yields identical output.

## Acceptance Criteria

- All functionality explicitly described above is implemented fully; no scope reduction, no feature deletions, and no silent behavioral regressions.
- Code is modular, documented where needed, and integrated with existing crates/interfaces without compatibility shims or duplicated logic.
- Comprehensive automated tests are added/updated for this issue: focused unit tests + relevant integration coverage for touched behavior and edge cases.
- End-to-end validation is covered where applicable (CLI and/or WASM path) with detailed structured logging (inputs, detection results, node/edge counts, output sizes, timings, and pass/fail reasons).
- Diagnostics are actionable: failures/warnings include precise context and suggested remediation.
- Determinism and stability expectations are verified for affected outputs (layout/render/serialization as applicable).

 {"id":"bd-2xl.14","title":"Build automated failing-input minimizer for reproducible bug triage","description":"Implement an automatic failing-input minimizer to shrink reproduction cases for parser/layout/render bugs.

Scope:
1. Add reducer that minimizes .mmd inputs while preserving target failure signature.
2. Support failure signatures: panic, timeout, invalid output class, deterministic mismatch.
3. Integrate reducer into fuzz and adversarial test workflows.
4. Store minimized repro artifacts with trace bundles for triage.
5. Provide CLI utility command for manual reduction sessions.

Why this improves the system:
- Dramatically reduces debugging time for hard edge-case failures.

 {"id":"bd-2xl.15","title":"Add real-world Mermaid corpus ingestion and trend analysis pipeline","description":"Create a real-world corpus ingestion pipeline from public README mermaid snippets for compatibility tracking.

Scope:
1. Build a curated, license-safe corpus collector for Mermaid snippets from public repositories.
2. Normalize and deduplicate corpus inputs by diagram type and syntax profile.
3. Run nightly compatibility and rendering checks against corpus.
4. Publish trend reports: pass rate, warning rate, top failure signatures.
5. Feed high-value failures into regression fixture sets automatically.

Why this improves the system:
- Aligns roadmap priorities with real user inputs, not synthetic examples only.

Parser invariants: (1) parse never panics for any input string, (2) parse always returns IR (never Err, per error recovery design), (3) re-serializing IR and re-parsing produces equivalent IR (round-trip where applicable), (4) detected diagram type is consistent across multiple calls.

Layout invariants: (1) layout never panics, (2) all nodes have non-overlapping bounding boxes (within configurable tolerance), (3) all clusters contain their child nodes, (4) layout is deterministic (same input = same output, byte-identical), (5) quality metrics are non-negative.

Render invariants: (1) SVG output is valid XML, (2) SVG contains expected number of node/edge elements, (3) render never panics, (4) terminal render fits within specified dimensions.

 {"id":"bd-2xl.7","title":"Write comprehensive unit tests for fm-parser","description":"Write exhaustive unit tests for the parser. Test categories:

1. DIAGRAM TYPE DETECTION: test detect for every supported type keyword, misspelled keywords, missing keywords, ambiguous content. At least 3 test cases per diagram type.

2. FLOWCHART PARSER: basic A-->B, complex multi-node, all node shapes, all arrow types (-->, --->, -.->., ==>, --o, --x), subgraphs, direction keywords (TB/TD/LR/RL/BT), class definitions, click events, links, multi-line labels with |text|, quoted strings, special characters in IDs.

3. SEQUENCE PARSER: participants, actors, messages (solid/dashed/open), activations, notes (left/right/over), loops, alt/opt/par fragments, autonumber.

4. CLASS PARSER: class declarations, members (attributes+methods), relationships (inheritance <|-, composition *-, aggregation o-, etc.), cardinality, annotations/stereotypes.

5. STATE PARSER: states, transitions, start/end markers, nested states, fork/join, choice, notes.

6. GANTT PARSER: title, dateFormat, sections, tasks with dates/durations, dependencies (after), milestones, exclusions.

7. PIE PARSER: title, showData, slices with labels and values.

8. ERROR RECOVERY: malformed input never panics, dangling edges create placeholder nodes, fuzzy keyword matching works, mixed syntax detection, diagnostic messages are attached to IR.

9. DOT PARSER: digraph, graph, strict, attributes, subgraph, HTML labels, escape sequences.

Logging: each test case logs input string, detected type, parsed IR summary (node count, edge count), and any diagnostics. Use tracing::info! for all test logging.

Target: 200+ tests in fm-parser. Every diagram type must have positive AND negative test cases.

 {"id":"bd-2xl.8","title":"Write comprehensive unit tests for fm-layout algorithms","description":"Write exhaustive unit tests for all layout algorithms. Test categories:

1. SUGIYAMA PIPELINE (per phase):
   a. Cycle removal: DAG (no cycles, no reversals), single cycle (A->B->C->A), nested cycles, self-loop, large SCC. Verify: reversed edge count, edge list matches, deterministic output.
   b. Rank assignment: linear chain (ranks 0,1,2...), diamond shape (A->B,A->C,B->D,C->D), wide graph. Verify: rank ordering respects edges, no rank gaps.
   c. Crossing minimization: known-optimal layout (verify 0 crossings), deliberately crossed layout (verify reduction). Verify: crossing count strictly non-increasing per iteration.
   d. Coordinate assignment: verify spacing constraints, label width expansion, no overlap. Verify: DiagramLayout.bounds encloses all nodes.

2. DETERMINISM: run same graph 100 times, verify byte-identical DiagramLayout output every time. Use proptest with fixed seeds.

3. FORCE-DIRECTED: small graph (5 nodes), medium (50 nodes), disconnected components. Verify: no node overlap, connected nodes closer than disconnected, deterministic from same input.

4. TREE LAYOUT: binary tree, unbalanced tree, forest (multiple roots). Verify: parent above children, no sibling overlap, correct orientation (TB/LR/RL/BT).

5. EDGE CASES: empty graph (0 nodes), single node, two disconnected nodes, fully connected K5, star graph, long chain (100 nodes).

6. QUALITY METRICS: verify LayoutStats fields are populated and sensible (crossings >= 0, iterations > 0, etc.).

Logging: each test logs graph description, algorithm used, iteration count, final crossing count, total edge length, and elapsed time. Use tracing::info! with structured fields.

Target: 100+ tests in fm-layout.

 {"id":"bd-2xl.9","title":"Write comprehensive unit tests for fm-render-svg","description":"Write exhaustive unit tests for SVG rendering. Test categories:

1. SVG DOCUMENT: valid XML output (parse with quick-xml or roxmltree), correct xmlns, viewBox computed from content, responsive width/height, embedded style element.

2. SHAPES: every NodeShape variant produces valid SVG path elements. Test: Rect (4 corners), Rounded (rx/ry attributes), Circle (cx/cy/r), Diamond (4-point polygon), Hexagon (6-point), Stadium (pill path), Cylinder (ellipse + rect), all shape-specific tests. Verify: label text centered within shape bounds, CSS class attributes present.

3. EDGES: solid/dashed/dotted/thick styles all render correctly. Arrowhead markers defined in defs. Bezier curves vs polylines. Self-loops. Parallel edges. Back-edges (from cycle breaking) get distinct styling.

4. CLUSTERS: rounded rect with correct bounds, label positioning, nested cluster z-order, padding.

5. THEMING: every preset theme produces distinct color values. CSS custom properties present. Override mechanism works. Dark/light mode switching.

6. TEXT: multi-line labels split correctly. Unicode text (CJK, emoji, RTL). Long labels truncated. Markdown bold/italic rendering.

7. ACCESSIBILITY: role attributes present, title/desc elements, tabindex on interactive elements.

8. SVG VALIDATION: parse every test output with an XML parser to verify well-formedness. No unclosed tags, no invalid attributes.

Logging: each test logs diagram description, SVG byte size, element counts (nodes, edges, clusters, text elements), and any validation errors. Use insta crate for snapshot testing where applicable.

Target: 80+ tests in fm-render-svg.

+{"id":"bd-ml2r","title":"[FNX] EPIC: Graph Intelligence Integration via franken_networkx","description":"Background\nfrankenmermaid already has deterministic parse->layout->render pipelines. franken_networkx (fnx) provides a high-performance algorithm engine plus CGSE witness artifacts. Integrating the two can materially improve structural diagnostics, layout decision quality, and observability if we stage the rollout carefully.\n\nCritical Constraint\nCurrent Rust fnx algorithms are primarily Graph (undirected) centric. Immediate wins should target undirected structural analysis and diagnostics. Directed-layout replacement must be treated as a later phase gated by explicit DiGraph coverage in fnx.\n\nEpic Goal\nAdd an accretive fnx-powered graph-intelligence layer to frankenmermaid that:\n1) improves diagnostics and layout decisions,\n2) preserves deterministic behavior,\n3) exposes explainable evidence/witness artifacts,\n4) avoids risky big-bang replacement of fm-layout internals.\n\nProgram Outcomes\n- Better parser/CLI diagnostics (components, articulation points, bridges, cycle basis).\n- Better layout tie-breaks and cycle-reversal scoring on dense/pathological graphs.\n- Witness-aware telemetry for algorithmic decisions and complexity costs.\n- Feature-gated rollout with deterministic parity checks and explicit success criteria.\n\nDefinition of Done\n- All child issues complete with acceptance criteria and test evidence.\n- Determinism checks pass repeatedly for representative corpus.\n- Quality gates pass (, , ) using rch offload.\n- CLI/WASM surfaces expose actionable fnx-derived diagnostics and decision traces.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-02-27T21:51:05.701089257Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-27T21:51:05.701089257Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["architecture","diagnostics","fnx","layout","parser"]}
+{"id":"bd-ml2r.1","title":"[FNX] Integration architecture contract and phased risk matrix","description":"Goal
Create the authoritative technical contract for integrating franken_networkx (fnx) into frankenmermaid without destabilizing current deterministic behavior.

Background
The project already has deterministic parsing/layout/rendering in Rust. fnx provides strong graph algorithms and witness artifacts but current Rust APIs are largely undirected. We need a staged plan that captures immediate wins and blocks unsafe assumptions.

Scope
- Define hard boundaries between Phase 1 (undirected structural intelligence) and Phase 2 (directed algorithm adoption).
- Define success metrics for quality, latency, determinism, and diagnostics usefulness.
- Define explicit rollback conditions and kill-switch behavior via feature flags.
- Produce implementation map across crates: fm-core, fm-parser, fm-layout, fm-cli, fm-wasm.

Acceptance Criteria
- Written architecture contract embedded in repo docs and referenced by all child beads.
- Determinism contract and fallback behavior explicitly specified.
- Risk register includes algorithm mismatch, runtime overhead, and maintenance coupling.
- Clear go/no-go gates for each integration phase.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-27T21:52:35.873613155Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-27T21:52:35.873613155Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["architecture","fnx","planning"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-ml2r.1","depends_on_id":"bd-ml2r","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-27T21:52:35.873613155Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}

 {"id":"bd-u785","title":"EPIC: Killer Demo — Layout Regression Test Harness","description":"## Killer Demo — Layout Regression Test Harness

Build an automated visual regression testing harness that renders the complete FrankenMermaid test corpus to SVG/PNG, compares against golden reference images using perceptual diff, and produces a browsable HTML report. This serves dual purpose: regression detection AND an impressive demo of FrankenMermaid's rendering capabilities.

## Motivation
Layout algorithms are notoriously difficult to regression-test with unit tests alone. A pixel-level change in coordinate assignment can propagate to produce visually different (but structurally correct) layouts. Conversely, a bug might produce structurally incorrect output that looks "close enough" to pass manual review. The regression harness provides:
1. **Automated comparison:** perceptual diff between current and golden renders
2. **Browsable report:** HTML gallery showing before/after/diff for each test case
3. **Demo showcase:** the report itself IS the demo — browse all diagram types, see FrankenMermaid's quality
4. **Regression budget:** configure acceptable diff threshold (e.g., 0.1% pixel difference) per diagram type

## Perceptual Diff Algorithm
Use structural similarity index (SSIM) or perceptual hash (pHash) rather than pixel-exact comparison:
- **SSIM:** Compares luminance, contrast, and structure. Score from 0 to 1, where 1 = identical.
  SSIM(x,y) = [l(x,y)]^α * [c(x,y)]^β * [s(x,y)]^γ
  where l = luminance, c = contrast, s = structure comparison
- **pHash:** DCT-based perceptual hash. Hamming distance between hashes measures visual similarity.
- **Threshold:** SSIM > 0.99 = no regression, 0.95-0.99 = warning, < 0.95 = regression

## Implementation
1. Create `regression-harness/` binary crate in workspace.
2. Input: directory of .mmd files (test corpus) + golden reference renders.
3. Pipeline per file: parse → layout → render to SVG → rasterize to PNG (via resvg or similar).
4. Compare each PNG against golden reference using SSIM.
5. Generate HTML report: thumbnail grid, click to expand, before/after/diff overlay.
6. Golden reference update: `cargo run --bin regression-harness -- --update-goldens`.
7. CI integration: run on every PR that touches fm-layout or fm-render, fail if any SSIM < threshold.
8. Include timing data per render in the report (performance regression detection).

## Demo Aspect
The regression report doubles as the project's visual showcase:
- Categories: flowcharts, sequence diagrams, class diagrams, state diagrams, gantt charts, etc.
- Each category shows 5-10 representative diagrams at various complexities.
- Report includes metadata: node count, edge count, layout time, render time.
- Hosted version can be published to frankenmermaid.com/gallery for public demo.

## Success Metrics
- All diagram types in test corpus rendered and compared automatically
- SSIM threshold tuned to catch real regressions while ignoring sub-pixel jitter
- HTML report loads in < 2 seconds with 500+ thumbnails
- Golden update workflow is one command + explicit commit
- CI gate blocks merge on regression (SSIM < 0.95)
- Report generation completes in < 5 minutes for full corpus

## Dependencies
- Requires fm-render-svg (bd-1y5) for SVG output
- Requires test corpus (bd-2xl.1 golden test infrastructure)","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:45:39.374613334Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-13T17:30:18.947294342Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["demo","layout","regression","testing","visual"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-u785","depends_on_id":"bd-17e4.2","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-13T17:30:18.947218691Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-u785","depends_on_id":"bd-1y5","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:45:44.324472620Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-u785","depends_on_id":"bd-2xl.1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:45:44.427880609Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"bd-vb9","title":"EPIC: Advanced Layout Algorithms","description":"Upgrade fm-layout with superior layout algorithms that produce significantly better-looking diagrams than mermaid-js. The existing Sugiyama implementation is solid but we need: (1) dramatically improved cycle handling that doesn't just reverse edges but produces aesthetically pleasing layouts, (2) force-directed layout for organic/non-hierarchical diagrams, (3) tree layout for mindmaps/org charts, (4) specialized layouts for timeline/gantt/sankey/kanban/grid diagrams, (5) constraint-based refinement for fine-tuning. The layout engine is the single biggest factor in diagram quality -- this is where we win against mermaid-js. All algorithms must maintain the determinism guarantee: identical input always produces identical output.

## Success Criteria

- Release readiness is proven by passing workspace quality gates and epic-level validation flows end-to-end.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-02-11T16:31:28.678967346Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-12T01:49:47.883609448Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["epic","layout"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-vb9","depends_on_id":"bd-3bc.3","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-12T08:37:48Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}],"comments":[{"id":42,"issue_id":"bd-vb9","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"Idea-wizard expansion: added bd-vb9.8 (layout time-budget guardrails + deterministic fallback) to protect reliability on pathological graphs.","created_at":"2026-02-12T01:46:27Z"}]}
 {"id":"bd-vb9.1","title":"Implement superior cycle handling in Sugiyama layout","description":"This is THE core differentiator mentioned in the X thread. Mermaid-js handles cycles poorly. Our approach:

1. DETECTION: Tarjan's SCC algorithm to find all strongly connected components. Report cycle count, participants, and nesting depth.

2. MULTI-STRATEGY CYCLE BREAKING (user-configurable):
   a. GREEDY (current): Source/sink peeling. Fast, good for mostly-DAG graphs.
   b. DFS-BACK: Depth-first search, reverse back edges. Minimizes reversed edges.
   c. MINIMUM-FEEDBACK-ARC-SET: Approximation algorithm (NP-hard exact). Finds near-minimal set of edges to reverse. Better aesthetics for heavily cyclic graphs.
   d. CYCLE-AWARE: Don't break cycles at all -- render them as visible cycles with curved back-edges. This is the 'honest' approach that shows the user their cycles rather than hiding them.

3. BACK-EDGE RENDERING: For reversed edges in GREEDY/DFS/MFAS modes, provide metadata so renderers can draw them distinctly (dashed line, different color, reversed arrowhead) to communicate that these represent cycles.

4. CYCLE CLUSTER COLLAPSE: Option to collapse an entire SCC into a single 'cycle cluster' node that can be expanded. Reduces visual complexity for highly cyclic subgraphs.

5. QUALITY METRICS: Track number of reversed edges, total edge length increase from reversal, visual symmetry of cycle representation. Expose in LayoutStats.

Determinism: All strategies must be deterministic given identical input. Use stable sorting, break ties by node ID.

## Acceptance Criteria

- All functionality explicitly described above is implemented fully; no scope reduction, no feature deletions, and no silent behavioral regressions.
- Code is modular, documented where needed, and integrated with existing crates/interfaces without compatibility shims or duplicated logic.
- Comprehensive automated tests are added/updated for this issue: focused unit tests + relevant integration coverage for touched behavior and edge cases.
- End-to-end validation is covered where applicable (CLI and/or WASM path) with detailed structured logging (inputs, detection results, node/edge counts, output sizes, timings, and pass/fail reasons).
- Diagnostics are actionable: failures/warnings include precise context and suggested remediation.
- Determinism and stability expectations are verified for affected outputs (layout/render/serialization as applicable).
- Quality gates pass: `cargo fmt --check`, `cargo clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings`, `cargo check --all-targets`, and relevant `cargo test` scope.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-11T16:31:50.539611823Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-22T01:33:49.344910966Z","closed_at":"2026-02-22T01:33:49.343029732Z","close_reason":"Completed: added 12 comprehensive cycle strategy tests, SCC cycle cluster collapse with LayoutConfig/CycleClusterMap/LayoutCycleCluster, and edge length quality metrics (reversed_edge_total_length, total_edge_length). All 27 fm-layout tests pass, clippy clean across workspace.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["cycles","differentiator","layout"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-vb9.1","depends_on_id":"bd-3bc.3","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-12T08:37:48Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-vb9.1","depends_on_id":"bd-vb9","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-12T08:37:48Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
